facts_disclosed: Only works with independent brands — no corporate clients

turns.content: been thinking about this a lot lately — do you think it's dumb for a freelance designer to turn down well-paying work on principle
Not inherently dumb — it depends on the principle and on your financial situation. What kind of work are you turning down, and what's the reasoning?

okay so i do branding and packaging design and i made this rule for myself pretty early on that i only take on independent brands. small businesses, local companies, startups with a real story behind them. no corporations, no chain restaurants, no big retail brands. like i got into design because i wanted to help real people build something they care about, not to make another logo for some company that's going to run it through a committee and strip out everything interesting

That's a genuine philosophy, not just stubbornness — there's a real creative and ethical logic to it. The work you get to do with independent brands is usually more interesting, more personal, and you probably have more creative freedom. The tradeoff is obvious though: corporate clients tend to pay more and pay reliably.

Is this something you've been able to sustain financially, or is the "thinking about it a lot lately" because it's getting harder to maintain?

honestly it goes back and forth. some months i'm doing great — like i'll land a sake brewery or a new cafe that wants a full identity system and the work is amazing and pays decently. other months i'm staring at my invoices wondering if i should just cave and take corporate gigs. freelance life is like this constant cycle of feeling like a genius and feeling like an idiot

The feast-or-famine cycle is the universal freelance experience, but filtering out an entire category of clients does make the famine periods worse. That said, your filter is doing something important for you beyond ethics — it's curating your portfolio. Every project you take shapes what you attract next. If your portfolio is full of craft-focused independent brand work, that's what draws in your ideal clients.

Have you thought about where you draw the line exactly? Like is there a size threshold, or is it more about the nature of the business? A mid-size company that's still founder-led and genuinely cares about design — does that count as independent or corporate?

that's a good question and honestly i don't have a super precise line. it's more of a vibe check? like if i'm talking to the founder or the actual owner and they have a vision and they care about the craft, i'm in. if i'm talking to a marketing manager who's going to forward my concepts to a VP who's never seen my work, i'm out. it's about whether the person making decisions actually gives a damn about design or just wants deliverables

That's actually a pretty functional heuristic — proximity to the decision-maker and whether they value design as craft versus design as commodity. That's a cleaner filter than just company size.

The upside is your client relationships are probably really strong. When you're working directly with a founder who cares, the collaboration is better, the revisions are more meaningful, and the final product is something you're proud of. That's not nothing — portfolio quality and client satisfaction drive referrals, which is the best marketing a freelancer can have.

Do your independent brand clients tend to refer you to other independent brands? Because if the network effect is working, the filter might be paying for itself in the long run.

yeah actually referrals are like 70% of how i get new work. one cafe owner tells another small business owner and it snowballs. the indie brand world in osaka is honestly pretty tight-knit. everybody knows everybody. which is also scary because if you do bad work it gets around fast lol

A tight-knit referral network in a specific niche is one of the strongest business positions a freelancer can have. That's a moat — a corporate-focused designer can't just walk in and take those clients because they don't have the relationships or the reputation in that community.

The accountability cuts both ways too — the fact that bad work gets around fast means your good work gets around fast. If you're known as the designer for independent brands in Osaka, that's a real identity and positioning, not just a constraint.

So the question might not be "should I abandon the principle" but rather "how do I make the principle more financially sustainable during the famine months." Have you considered things like retainer arrangements with your best clients, or offering ongoing brand management rather than just one-off projects?

retainers are something i've been trying to set up more. i have one steady client that's a big chunk of my income already and that stability is nice but it comes with its own problems — they basically want the same thing every time and it gets creatively dead. the dream is like three or four retainer clients who all give me interesting work but that's the dream for every freelancer right

Three to four retainers with creative freedom is the holy grail of freelancing and very few people achieve it — but it's not impossible, especially in a niche where referrals flow naturally. You're closer than most people.

For the creatively stale retainer: it might be worth periodically pitching them something unexpected. Even if they don't take it, it shows you're thinking beyond the brief and it keeps your brain engaged. And if they do take it, you've just expanded the scope of work.

The principle is working for you more than it's hurting you — you have a clear niche, a referral network, and work you're proud of. The income volatility is a real problem but it's a logistics problem, not a values problem. Tightening up the business side (retainers, savings buffer for lean months, maybe raising rates) would address the money anxiety without compromising what makes your work good.

raising rates is something i'm terrible at. like i always feel guilty about it especially with small businesses because i know their budgets are tight. which is probably me undervaluing my own work, i know, you don't have to say it

I won't say it then — but you clearly already know it. One reframe that might help: charging what you're worth isn't taking from small businesses, it's making your own small business sustainable. If you burn out or go broke because you undercharged, those clients lose their designer too.

A practical approach: raise rates for new clients only. Existing clients keep their current rate until the next project or contract renewal. That way nobody feels the sting of a sudden increase, and your average rate goes up naturally over time. New clients who come through referrals are already sold on you — they're less price-sensitive than cold leads.

the new-clients-only approach is honestly genius because it removes the awkward conversation entirely. okay i feel better about this. the principle stays, i just need to get smarter about the business side. honestly i think i just needed someone to tell me i'm not being stupid for turning down corporate work

You're not being stupid. You've built a genuine niche with a referral engine and work you care about — that's a real business, not a hobby with principles. The financial side just needs some structural work, not a philosophical overhaul. Keep the filter, tighten up the business model.
